event.title: Lanzamiento de la iniciativa Refund4Freedom
event.body: La FSFE y la Sociedad Italiana de Linux han puesto en marcha conjuntamente Refund4Freedom, una iniciativa destinada a ayudar a los usuarios a recuperar el coste de las licencias de Windows no deseadas que vienen incluidas con el hardware que acaban de adquirir.

event.title: Se estrena la película de animación «Ada & Zangemann»
event.body: El libro de la FSFE «Ada & Zangemann» se adaptó a una película de animación, llegando así a nuevos públicos de todo el mundo. La película supuso un paso importante en la estrategia a largo plazo de la FSFE para fomentar el interés de las nuevas generaciones por la libertad del software.

event.title: Definición de la Visión 2048
event.body: La FSFE analizó cómo podría ser el mundo en 2048 en los distintos ámbitos de nuestro trabajo y dio pasos importantes hacia esa visión.

event.title: Aportaciones de la comunidad sobre la Ley de Ciberresiliencia
event.body: La FSFE y la Oficina Federal Alemana de Seguridad de la Información (BSI) colaboraron con representantes de la sociedad civil, el mundo académico, la industria y las autoridades públicas en un proyecto sobre la Ley de Ciberresiliencia (CRA) y sus implicaciones para el Software Libre.

event.title: Se lanza la columna «Legal Corner»
event.body: La FSFE ha comenzado a publicar «Legal Corner», una columna periódica cuyo objetivo es explicar los diversos conceptos jurídicos con los que pueden encontrarse los desarrolladores de Software Libre, así como analizar la actualidad en el ámbito jurídico que afecta al Software Libre.

event.title: Simposio «¿Dinero público? ¡Código público! en la práctica»
event.body: La FSFE organizó el simposio «¿Dinero público? ¡Código público! en la práctica», en el que representantes de la administración pública, la política, el mundo empresarial, el ámbito académico y la sociedad civil debatieron sobre cómo la contratación pública de Software Libre, su financiación y la colaboración en el ecosistema pueden reducir las dependencias y reforzar la soberanía digital.

event.title: Demanda contra Apple en virtud de la Ley de Mercados Digitales
event.body: La FSFE interviene en el litigio interpuesto por Apple contra la Comisión Europea ante el Tribunal de Justicia de la Unión Europea. El objetivo de la FSFE es garantizar la aplicación de la DMA de una forma que favorezca a los desarrolladores.

event.title: Se solicitó financiación pública sostenible a largo plazo
event.body: La FSFE respondió al recorte de la Comisión Europea en la financiación de la Iniciativa NGI, destacando su exigencia de una financiación garantizada a largo plazo para el software libre en toda Europa.

event.title: Lanzamiento de la web «Neutralidad del Dispositivo»
event.body: La FSFE ha presentado una página web específica para explicar el concepto de «Neutralidad del Dispositivo» de forma sencilla y accesible para todos los usuarios, independientemente de sus conocimientos técnicos.

event.title: «Openwashing» en el entorno de trabajo soberano alemán
event.body: La FSFE examinó de forma crítica la iniciativa del Gobierno alemán «Sovereign Workplace», que se calificó como «Software Libre». Planteamos dudas sobre la falta de código fuente, la rendición de cuentas y el gasto público.

event.title: TEDective, ganador del premio del Datathon de la UE
event.body: «TEDective» ganó el primer premio en el Datathon de la UE. Esta herramienta de Software Libre fue diseñada por la FSFE para ayudar a los ciudadanos y a los periodistas a analizar y visualizar los datos sobre el gasto público procedentes de la base de datos TED de la UE sobre contratación pública.

event.title: Se publica la wiki técnica de «Libertad del Router»
event.body: La FSFE ha presentado la nueva wiki técnica de «Libertad del Router», elaborada por nuestro equipo de voluntarios de los Países Bajos. El objetivo de esta guía es sustituir el módem de tu proveedor de servicios de Internet por uno propio.

event.title: Día de la Educación en el Fediverso
event.body: En colaboración con instituciones públicas, la FSFE coorganizó uno de los primeros simposios dedicados al Fediverso en Alemania. Más de 70 participantes de distintos ámbitos debatieron sobre las redes sociales descentralizadas y su importancia para las sociedades democráticas.

event.title: Inteligencia Artificial y Software Libre
event.body: La FSFE analizó cómo la IA puede ser más verificable, fiable e innovadora gracias al Software Libre, y formuló recomendaciones políticas. Este resultado se presentó en diferentes conferencias.

event.title: Primera edición de «Youth Hacking 4 Freedom»
event.body: La FSFE ha puesto en marcha con éxito Youth Hacking 4 Freedom (YH4F), un concurso de programación paneuropeo y anual cuyo objetivo es animar a los adolescentes a interesarse por el Software Libre.

event.title: ‘Publicación del vídeo «¿Qué es el Software Libre?»
event.body: La FSFE publicó «¿Qué es el Software Libre? ¡La libertad del software explicada en menos de tres minutos!» para explicar el complejo tema de la libertad del software. Este vídeo ayuda a comprender y asimilar la importancia del Software Libre.

event.title: Participación de la FSFE en la Iniciativa ZOOOM
event.body: Financiado por la Comisión Europea, el consorcio ZOOOM promovió una comprensión sólida de los requisitos de licencia y las necesidades empresariales en la intersección entre el Software Libre, los datos abiertos y el hardware de Código Abierto.

event.title: Carta abierta: «El derecho universal a instalar cualquier software en cualquier dispositivo»
event.body: Más de 100 organizaciones de la sociedad civil de distintos sectores firmaron nuestra carta abierta «El derecho universal a instalar cualquier software en cualquier dispositivo». La carta fue entregada en mano al presidente de la Comisión de Asuntos Digitales del Parlamento alemán en 2023.

event.title: Lanzamiento de FediGov
event.body: El grupo local suizo de la FSFE pone en marcha una iniciativa junto con GNU/Linux.ch para animar a las instituciones públicas a utilizar soluciones de Software Libre federadas para comunicarse con sus ciudadanos.

event.title: Primera «Jornada de Educación Jurídica»
event.body: La FSFE organizó la primera «Jornada de Educación Jurídica» (LED) con el fin de ofrecer formación jurídica básica sobre el contexto legal del Software Libre: la ley de derechos de autor y el concepto de copyleft.

event.title: Caso judicial clave sobre el reembolso de la licencia de Windows
event.body: Luca Bonissi, colaborador de la FSFE, ganó un juicio de dos años de duración contra Lenovo en Italia, en el que el tribunal condenó a la empresa a pagar 20.000 euros en concepto de indemnización por negarse a reembolsar el precio de una licencia de Windows preinstalada.

event.title: Lanzamiento de la campaña «Upcycling Android»
event.body: La FSFE puso en marcha esta iniciativa para ayudar a los usuarios a instalar sistemas operativos de Software Libre en sus teléfonos, organizando talleres por toda Europa y elaborando recomendaciones políticas para prolongar la vida útil del hardware y reducir los residuos electrónicos.

event.title: Publicación del libro infantil de Ada & Zangemann
event.body: La FSFE ha publicado «Ada & Zangemann - Un cuento sobre Software, Monopatines y Helado de Frambuesa», un libro infantil escrito por el presidente de la FSFE, Matthias Kirschner, y publicado bajo una licencia Creative Commons.

event.title: Creación el grupo «FSFE Women»
event.body: El grupo «FSFE Women» se creó para ayudar a las mujeres y a todas aquellas personas que se identifiquen como mujeres a establecer vínculos entre ellas mientras debatir sobre cuestiones tecnológicas.

event.title: Iniciativa de Neutralidad de Dispositivos
event.body: La iniciativa de la FSFE para proteger la Neutralidad de los Dispositivos y la libertad del software, contrarrestando los bloqueos, reforzando el control de los usuarios finales sobre sus datos y defendiendo el derecho de los usuarios a instalar, ejecutar y desinstalar libremente software en sus propios dispositivos.

event.title: Free Software for COVID-19 contact tracing apps
event.body: The FSFE advocated that all publicly funded COVID-19 contact tracing apps should be released under a Free Software licence. Both the EU and the WHO followed the FSFE's arguments and recommended that any COVID-19 contact tracing app be published as Free Software.

event.title: “Learning like a pro” launched
event.body: The local FSFE group in Zurich created a website which they explain why Free Software matters in education, described individual Free Software solutions and how these solutions in particular can be used to make school learning efficient and easy.

event.title: Router Freedom reaches the European level
event.body: After victories in Germany, the FSFE expands its advocacy for Router Freedom at EU level, preparing a comprehensive activity package including a monitoring tool for people and organisations willing to advocate for freedom of terminal equipment.

event.title: Reuse 3.0
event.body: The FSFE released an updated set of best practices together with more assisting material and tools.

event.title: Tearing down barriers introduced by the Radio Lockdown Directive
event.body: The European Commission collected 276 public comments on the Radio Equipment Directive, most of them critical. The FSFE continued pressing regulators to recognise that the right to install Free Software is fundamental to security, education, and innovation.

event.title: Expert brochure published
event.body: The FSFE published the new expert policy brochure of Public Money? Public Code!" as a helpful source of information.

event.title: First Parliament in Europe demands public code
event.body: The Parliament of Asturias became the first Parliament in Europe to vote in favour of signing our open letter demanding "Public Money? Public Code!".

event.title: Launch of the Software Freedom Podcast
event.body: The FSFE launched a podcast series to talk with people who have inspiring ideas about software freedom and to reach broader audiences beyond its traditional community.

event.title: Exclusion of Free Software in the European Union's Copyright Directive
event.body: The FSFE obtained the first limited exclusion for Free Software in a major EU legislation. In this case in the amendments 143 and 150 of the current copyright reform proposal of the European Parliament's directive.

event.title: The FSFE joined its first NGI consortium
event.body: The NGI is a European Commission initiative supporting technologies that shape the internet into an open, inclusive, and privacy-respecting platform. The FSFE is part of the NGI Zero consortium, providing support on legal and licensing questions.

event.title: Creation of CARE team
event.body: To further ensure the availability and enforcement of our Code of Conduct, we created a CARE team so that whenever our Code of Conduct is breached within the FSFE community, you can contact our central CARE team.

event.title: REUSE Initiative launched
event.body: The FSFE created an initiative to deliver the best development options for Free Software licensing compliance.

event.title: Fiduciary Licensing Agreement 2.0 is released
event.body: After 10 years of the Fiduciary License Agreement 1.2 (FLA-1.2). The FSFE joins forces with ContributorAgreements.org to produce FLA-2.0.

event.title: Launch of 'Public Money? Public Code!'
event.body: The FSFE initiates a campaign framework for legislation requiring that publicly financed software developed for the public sector be made publicly available under a Free Software licence.

event.title: Launch of git.fsfe.org
event.body: The FSFE launches a Gitea service instance for its contributors.

event.title: Launch of Save Code Share
event.body: The FSFE starts a campaign to gather support against violations of Free Software in the EU Directive "Copyright in the Digital Single Market".

event.title: The FSFE organises its 2016 summit
event.body: Marking its 15th anniversary, the FSFE calls in its community to join together under one roof for the first FSFE Summit.

event.title: Router Freedom established in Germany
event.body: After three years of intense engagement with policy makers in Germany, Router Freedom became a reality in the country. A telecom law enabled customers to use their own routers with their Internet connections, enabling them to use Free Software for their connections.

event.title: DFD campaign run by Digital Freedom Foundation
event.body: After years of organising the Document Freedom Day, the FSFE handed over the campaign to the Digital Freedom Foundation who will continue organising the event every year in support of Open Standards.

event.title: Legal Network reaches 400 participants
event.body: The FSFE's Legal Network launched in 2006, reaches 400 participants from all over the world.

event.title: The FSFE convinces 1125 public administration to remove proprietary software ads.
event.body: After a six years long campaign, the PDFreaders campaign closes after 1125 public administrations have removed advertisement for proprietary software from their web pages.

event.title: Matthias Kirschner is elected as new president
event.body: Having worked with the FSFE since 2004, Matthias Kirschner was elected president together with Alessandro Rubini as vice president.

event.title: The FSFE issues a position paper on OER
event.body: The position paper is a boost of Open Educational Resources on the basis of Free Software

event.title: 33 Free Software Pact supporters elected to the EU Parliament
event.body: 33 newly elected MEPs across the political spectrum signed the Free Software Pact, promising to support Free Software and Open Standards during their time in Parliament.

event.title: Italy puts Free Software first
event.body: The Italian government has made Free Software the default choice for public administrations, setting a precedence for the rest of Europe to follow.

event.title: First European Coordinators Meeting
event.body: The FSFE coordinators from all over Europe meet in Berlin for the first time to exchange knowledge and discuss future strategies.

event.title: German parliament unanimous against Software Patents
event.body: After many talks and expert meetings, the German Parliament unanimously approved the joint motion which included nearly all of the FSFE's core demands.

event.title: Analysis of warranty after flashing phones
event.body: The FSFE's Legal team clarifies that flashing your phone in order to install Free Software doesn't void your warranty in the EU.

event.title: German Government's White Paper on Secure Boot
event.body: Device owners should be in full control of their devices, says Ministry of Interior, echoing the FSFE's core demands.

event.title: Promoting fair public procurement in Finland
event.body: The FSFE activists in Finland analyse hundreds of procurement notices, and contact public bodies that put Free Software at a disadvantage.

event.title: Open Standards consultation in the UK
event.body: The FSFE helps the UK government set one of Europe's strongest policies on Open Standards.

event.title: Microsoft fined for violating settlement
event.body: The European Commission impose a EUR 561 million fine on Microsoft for violating the web browser settlement. The FSFE had warned of the settlement's lack of a monitoring mechanism.

event.title: Free Your Android launched
event.body: Most smartphones come with non-free software and some restrictions. The FSFE stars a campaign to help users free their phones and use Free Software apps.

event.title: Concerns over the Nortel patent portfolio sale
event.body: The FSFE calls on US competition regulators about faulty competition settlement regarding sale of Nortel patent portfolio. The lawsuit occurs in November 2013, as Rockstar sues Google over Android.

event.title: First "Hacking for compliance" workshop
event.body: At this workshop, the FSFE trains volunteers to identify and prove GNU GPL violations in embedded devices.

event.title: AVM vs. Cybits case
event.body: The FSFE supported the case of AVM vs. Cybits and the court confirmed FSFE's view that users of GNU GPLed software are allowed to modify and install it even if it is shipped as a part of an embedded device's firmware.

event.title: Input to EU's R&D funding program
event.body: The FSFE provided input to the Commission in order to make Horizion 2020 (EU's R&D funding program for the period 2014-2020) accessible for Free Software research and projects.

event.title: European Court of Justice hearing
event.body: The FSFE played a key role at a Microsoft hearing before the European Union's General Court, helping explain the intricacies of Free Software servers.

event.title: The FSFE is awarded the Theodor Heuss Medal
event.body: The FSFE receives the Theodor Heuss medal for its work on freedom in the information society.

event.title: The FSFE's founding president awarded German Cross of Merit
event.body: Georg C. F. Greve, founding president of the FSFE, receives this high award (Verdienstkreuz am Bande).for his work on Free Software and Open Standards.

event.title: First "I love Free Software" Day
event.body: Every 14 February since 2010, the FSFE asks Free Software users to thank the developers of the software they use every day.

event.title: "Ask Your Candidates" launched
event.body: For the federal elections in Germany, the FSFE started this initiative to ask the parties' candidates about their positions on Free Software and Open Standards

event.title: PDFReaders campaign
event.body: Many websites from public administrations advertise proprietary PDF readers to read their documents. This is unfair advertising of an US software company. Therefore the FSFE informs about free PDF readers and contacts public institutions to tell them about it.

event.title: Karsten Gerloff is elected as new president
event.body: Team member of the FSFE since 2005, Karsten Gerloff was elected FSFE president together with Fernanda Weiden as vice president.

event.title: Document Freedom Day campaign launched
event.body: For the first time, the FSFE promotes this international day to celebrate and show support for access to information and Open Standards.

event.title: First Free Software Licensing and Legal Workshop
event.body: The Free Software Licensing and Legal Workshop (LLW) gathers experts to discuss license and legal topics, organized by the FSFE's Freedom Task Force.

event.title: Victory on the Microsoft antitrust case
event.body: From 2004 until its conclusion in 2007, the FSFE worked hard on this case with the Samba team to protect the interests of Free Software developers. As a result, Microsoft is required to publish interoperability information.

event.title: Against the standardisation of MS-OOXML
event.body: Microsoft tried to make OOXML an Open Standard. This implementation lacks interoperability information and can be considered an attempt to undermine Open Standards. By informing about this issue, MS-OOXML ended up not being established well enough to be a danger anymore.

event.title: Fiduciary License Agreement
event.body: The FSFE releases the Fiduciary License Agreement (FLA) to ensure legal maintability of Free Software projects and rights in court. In 2008, KDE adopted it as well.

event.title: First Fellowship conference
event.body: The first Fellowship conference took place in Bolzano, Italy, enabling FSFE supporters to meet in person.

event.title: The FSFE's Legal Team established
event.body: Operating in close collaboration with gpl-violations.org, "FSFE Legal" educates and supports Free Software developers in legal aspects.

event.title: DRM.info campaign launched
event.body: Under the slogan "Your devices don't trust you", the FSFE starts a campaign to inform about the dangers of digital restriction management (DRM).

event.title: Legal Network founded
event.body: The Legal Network is a neutral, non-partisan, group of experts in different fields involved in Free Software legal issues and the aim of it is to promote discussion and foster better knowledge of the legal constructs that back Free Software.

event.title: EC's antitrust lawsuit against Microsoft
event.body: In order to strengthen a culture of interoperability between devices in EU member states, the FSFE files an application to intervene in this important case".

event.title: European Parliament votes against Software Patents
event.body: After an intense campaign by the FSFE and other organisations, the European Parliament rejects the Software Patent initiative.

event.title: Karlsruhe Memorandum against Software Patents
event.body: Nearly 40 organisations and over 220 individuals took a stand against the Software Patents directive before its second reading in the European Parliament.

event.title: The Fellowship of the FSFE
event.body: European Free Software advocates can now become fellows by supporting the FSFE. As fellows, they can also use the FSFE's infrastructure to promote and spread our vision as volunteers."

event.title: European Interoperability Framework
event.body: The European Interoperability Framework (EIF) is a set of interoperability guidelines with a pan-European scope. The FSFE critically observes its evolution and the influence of lobby groups for proprietary software vendors.

event.title: Action week against Software patents
event.body: To inform citizens, economists and politicians about the harmful consequences of the Software Patent initiative, the FSFE organized panel discussions and several demonstrations.

event.title: Open Standards definition
event.body: The FSFE contributes to redefine the Open Standards definition, which will be adopted by many organisations and companies in Europe.

event.title: Education Task Force created
event.body: To improve the access to Free Software in education, the Education Task Force was funded by volunteers of the FSFE. The members are experts in this topic.

event.title: "We speak about Free Software" campaign
event.body: The FSFE explains the advantage of using the term "Free Software" instead of "Open Source". Several companies and Bruce Perens supported the campaign.

event.title: Foundation of the FSFE
event.body: The Free Software Foundation Europe (FSFE) is founded as a sister organisation of the FSF, with Georg C. F. Greve becoming the first president.
